story:
Missy, a small white mouse with a big heart, woke her mother before dawn. It was champions day, and Silvermoss lay remade by snow—trees frosted, roots carpeted, the usual damp moss smell replaced by a crisp, clean chill. Their woven hut, no bigger than a picnic basket, felt roomy now that Missy’s eleven siblings lived elsewhere. She thought of Barney, the brother who had left with badger and rabbit friends to explore the wider realm. Like him, she wanted more than the normal life of a mouse. Today, she hoped to win the victor’s stump.

They sledged on bark to the camp’s center, where Amis, the single-tusked boar chief, called for the forty-four competitors. Giants gathered: wolves and bears, stags and horses, foxes—and one tiny mouse who had to dart between paws, claws, and hooves. The crowd formed a lopsided circle. Tywit, a charcoal wolf with twice the muscle of his pack, laughed down at her. Missy kept her eyes on the stump and smiled anyway.

Amis announced a new first game: Extreme Piñata, with Regi, the stern golden-brown stallion, now giddy with fun, wrapped in vines heavy with apples, grapes, and plums. Knock a single piece of fruit loose in sixty seconds to advance. Chaos followed. Fur, antlers, and tails collided. Missy, late and clueless, seized a heavy twig, rammed it into a rock, and flung herself into the air. THUD. Something green smacked her face. Dizzy in the snow, she watched a grape roll to her side. She placed it in the bucket with twenty-three others and advanced.

Next came teams by height—Amis paced down the line: blueberry, raspberry, blueberry… until Missy, a raspberry, stood glumly beside a fox. Tug of war. If any paw, claw, or hoof crossed into the other side, the whole team was out. Her teammates frowned, Tywit growled, and Missy stared at the thick rope. Balance, she told herself. When the pull began, the raspberries slid, inch by inch, toward defeat. Then Missy sprang up, steadied herself on the rope, and sprinted—head to head, ear to ear, antler to antler—sowing confusion without ever touching blueberry ground. The blueberries toppled like dominoes. The raspberries heaved the rope across. Protests erupted until Higgins, an old pig, silenced the uproar, and Regi coolly confirmed the rule: Missy had never set foot where she shouldn’t. Raspberries win. Missy was in the final twelve.

For the last game, Amis chose a snow fight—last animal standing. Bears packed boulders, wolves prepared to rake icy sheets, stags and horses readied their hind legs. Missy had no throw that could match them. She kept moving—jumping, skidding, twisting—her white coat a blur against the snow. Then she heard her mother cry, “The Tree!” Missy scrambled up a dying sycamore, scrabbling into a snow-loaded fork and clutching at the branch for balance. Names fell away. Soon, only three remained: Missy hidden, Milo the strongest bear, and Tywit, menacing and sure. Milo launched a giant snowball. Tywit leapt, charged, and swept a thick layer over the bear’s fur. “Milo, out!” The wolf strutted, claiming the stump and his victory.

Splat. A hard-packed clump of snow, shaken loose from the branch above, hit Tywit’s face. Every eye turned to the sycamore, where Missy, dangling by her tail, swung and stared. “Missy wins!” Amis cheered. Milo held her high. The camp roared: “Missy! Missy! Missy!” They built snow animals, shared warm raspberry pies, and sang by the fire. And later, under a thick blanket beside her mother, Missy watched a fresh, quiet snow begin to fall.
